Further Adventures with Adolf Grünbaum

Response to Grünbaum

Joseph Schwartz PHD

Joseph Schwartz

2 Lancaster Drive
London NW3 4HA
England
jadamschw@aol.com

Joseph Schwartz   Centre for Attachment-Based Psychoanalytic Psychotherapy, London, England.

On habermas and gadamer's important argument that psycho-analysis is fundamentally different from physics because the phenomena of physics are ahistorical and context independent, I emphasize that they are entirely correct. Physics deals with timeless forms of matter that appeared early in the evolution of the universe. As opposed to the phenomena of biology, the phenomena of physics are universal, independent of their location in space and time (context). One of the most important properties of the electron is that each electron is indistinguishable from all other electrons in the universe—a property that has as one consequence the periodic table of the chemical elements. The timeless universality of inanimate matter allows the construction of the great general laws of nature, of which physics can be justifiably proud.

As for Grünbaum's presentation of the physics of the so-called complete electromagnetic field and the hysteresis loop of a permanent magnet as counterexamples, I can in this space only reiterate the fact that there is no cumulative history in these systems as there is with DNA in its cellular environment. At any single point in time and space, the electromagnetic field created by a moving charge depends only on the position, velocity, and acceleration of the electric charge at a single previous time. Grünbaum's mistake is to assign historical significance to the fact that a specification of the electromagnetic field at every point in space at a given time—the so-called complete field—requires a knowledge of the past trajectory of the charge. At no point in space-time is the electric field cumulative.

The american dream in black and white: the clarence thomas hearings by Jane Flax (Ithaca, NY, Cornell University Press, 1998, 170 pp.)

In this brief but suggestive new book, jane flax, a professor of political science at Howard University and practicing psychotherapist, asks why “the American political system [has] been unable to eliminate…inequalities” of race and gender (pp. 1-2). She responds with a feminist critique of liberalism's and America's most cherished assumptions about fairness, the heroic (and heroically abstract) individual, the “problem” of difference. Although the main points of Flax's arguments will not be news to most readers versed in contemporary feminist and critical theories, her choice of method is refreshing. She focuses her critique through a case study of the Clarence Thomas hearings of September and October 1991 and uses the hearings to expose the American race/gender system and its effects and persistent inequalities. As I discuss here, her method is not uniformly successful. However, when it works, her case study method animates her theoretical points and grounds her suggestion that race/gender domination, far from representing historical anomalies, is an ongoing and, even more disturbingly, constitutive feature of the American dream.

Throughout The American Dream in Black and White, Flax writes race/gender as one word.

How We Institute Our Psychoanalytic Education

Reply to Commentaries

Randall Sorenson PHD Psy.D.

Randall Sorenson

100 North Hill Avenue, Suite 206
Pasadena, CA 91106
randysorenson@mac.com

Randall Sorenson  

I appreciate the thoughtful and differing commentaries by Drs. Bass, Druck, and Eisold. The points on which they differ can be viewed as helpful examples of the various ways that nearly all analysts will likely respond to the issues I raise about structuring our associations to facilitate psychoanalytic education. Because the argument in my essay progressed across three points (the idealization of science, imagining institutes as religious denominations, structuring psychoanalytic education), I return to each of these now as a way to continue the dialogue that the commentaries invite.

Idealization of Science

Philosopher Nancey Murphy (1990, 1997) has noted how the beginning and ending of the modern period—which in philosophy lasted from roughly 1650 until 1950—punctuate two turning points in epistemology. The first, which inaugurated modernity in the mid-17th century, entailed a rejection of medieval notions authority, whether feudal or ecclesiastical, in favor of a foundationalism that sought to reconstruct knowledge so as to establish a secure foundation from which to know everything else. The second turn, which occurred in the middle of the 20th century and is still in progress, involves a rejection of modernity's foundationalism in favor of a more holist epistemology.

I am grateful to James W. Jones, Ph.D., Psy.D. and Gary R. Sattler, Dr. theol., Psy.D., who offered insightful comments on an earlier version of this reply.

Tempting though it is to plunge straight in, a few preliminary remarks are in order, particularly when one is writing from the other side of the Atlantic.

Group analysis brings together psychology and sociology, psychoanalysis and society, the personal and the political. However, it does not do this as a completed theory that can be used to produce explanations of various individual or social actions. It is instead a process of analysis, which is never completed. Each explanation adds to a new whole which can then be further analyzed. Students of Hegel will immediately recognize the process.

Moreover, it is analysis of the group by the group, including the conductor. So, what I have to say about the Clinton affair is merely one contribution to the debate, which is in itself open to be interpreted and analyzed. It needs also to be understood as an English point of view.

Group analysis is particularly concerned with communication, so we need to look very hard at the communication through which we are informed, or misinformed, about this whole business. In an analytic group, we hear directly what others say, and we see them saying it. We know them directly, and we have time to get to know them. But a public figure is very much a persona. A persona, moreover, that is significantly constructed by her or his own public relations machine and the media on which we are all to some extent dependent for our information about what is going on in the world beyond our direct experience. responseSet.title: The Clinton Phenomenon
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinton Phenomenon

Michael Šebek CSC

Michael Šebek

2nd Medical Faculty
17000 Prague 7, Korunovacn\'ed 18, Czech Republic
msebek@terminal.cz

Michael Šebek   President, Czech Psychoanalytical Provisional Society, and Associate Professor of Medical Psychology and Medical Ethics, Charles University, 2nd Medical Faculty.

The clinton phenomenon creates conflict in those who observe it or engage in it. At first, one needs to overcome some cultural barrier. From the Czech perspective, it seems to be strange to set up a persecuting institution against the president. Czechs are not very eager to publicize the private sexual matters of representatives of the state. For instance, today it is known that the first president of Czechoslovakia, T. G. Masaryk, had mistresses until his very old age. These facts were and still are hidden with help of archives and their directors. The uncomfortable feelings that would spread if this were to be uncovered are part of conventional morality. Therefore, Clinton's case was observed in the Czech Republic with restraint. Although the attraction of uncovering primal scenes seems to be natural, some sublimated versions of this attraction into ordinary curiosity regulated by cultural values are generally preferred, and even more common. But the border between “perverse” and “normal” is not today so sharply marked.

Clinton is not an analytic case; therefore, he can be assessed only as “the Clinton phenomenon,” and his self-destructive behaviors belong to some other realm. The discovery and publication of his sexual adventures reveal him as an ordinary man, but at the same time the question arises about what we consciously and unconsciously expect from a powerful authority in the highest position. In ancient times, kings were close to gods; they were idealized as protectors and feared as persecutors. Some strong parental roles were delegated to them from those below. In democracy, all “kings” are far from gods, and they are not felt to be almighty, but still some parental protective and persecuting functions are projected onto them, together with some idealization that also involves desexualization.

315

responseSet.title: Who to Be or Not to Be?: Commentary on Papers on President Clinton's Impeachment
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Who to Be or Not to Be?

Commentary on Papers on President Clinton's Impeachment

Jay Frankel PHD

Jay Frankel

290 West 12th Street, 6B
New York, NY 10014
Drjfrankel@aol.com

Jay Frankel   Co-Director, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; and Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

We have been treated to a panel of brief, interesting, and very different papers, all taking as a starting point the events surrounding the impeachment of President Clinton. These papers take off in diverging directions, using these events to explore the dynamics of hypocrisy (Rangell), particular varieties of individual pathology (Bloland, Orfanos), social pathology (Chasseguet-Smirgel, Orfanos), the nature of leadership and the relation of leaders to society (Blackwell, Samuels, Šebek, Žižek), and the nature and effects of power (Berenstein, Bloland). We owe the president thanks for serving, if only unwittingly and unhappily, as a foil for these various explorations.

Here I shall describe how, despite the different directions in which these papers go, there may be an underlying set of concerns that unifies them—a set of concerns that will force us to consider how we expect ourselves and our fellows to behave in our dealings with other people. First, though, I take these papers on their own terms. Bloland, and to a lesser extent Orfanos, takes a diagnostic approach, seeing Clinton as an example of a person with a particular kind of individual pathology—the pathology inherent in great ambition. Along similar lines, though Berenstein does not diagnose Clinton, he discusses how power can corrupt human relationships, and he makes the extraordinary observation that often someone who exercises power does not realize he or she is doing so.

The authors who diagnose Clinton take a risk, speculating beyond what they know about him. As Berenstein, Blackwell, and Šebek remind us, Clinton is not our patient; we know him only as a persona.

327

Jung's Contribution to Psychoanalytic Thought

Donald Kalsched PHD

Donald Kalsched

23 Parkway Street, #3A
Katonah, NY 10536
donedkal@aol.com

Donald Kalsched   Clinical Psychologist and Jungian Psychoanalyst who practices in New York City and Katonah, New York He is Faculty and Supervisor, New York Jung Institute and Faculty Inter-regional Society of Jungian Analysts; Member, Division 39 of the American Psychological Association; and Dean, Jungian Studies Specialty, Westchester Institute for Training in Psychoanalysis and Psychotherapy, Mt. Kisco, New York.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Jung's most significant clinical contributions to analytic practice are:

His emphasis on the primacy of affect as the central organizing principle of psychic life—instead of the drives—and how this leads to an understanding of the psyche's inherent dissociability.

His insistence that psychoanalysis was best conducted in a “bipersonal field” in which both partners sit face-to-face and mutually influence the result.

His understanding of dreams and the symbolic expressions of fantasy life as images that give form to unconscious affects and hence are agents of evolving consciousness, not “compromise formations.” This led Jung to use various nonverbal techniques within the analytic situation, including drawing, painting, and active imagination.

His structural model (archetypes) of the psyche, which includes a “collective” or “psychoid” layer in which events occur that are not just psychological but psysical as well (echoing recent discoveries in both psychosomatics and atomic physics). This “magical” or “mystical” realm is the basis for religious experience and for synchronicity and other noncausal connective events otherwise known as “transgressive” or “paranormal.

This paper on Benjamin is part 1 of a series presenting the work of three contemporary theorists whose ideas are associated with the intersubjective turn in psychoanalysis. Part 2, on Bollas, and part 3, on Ehrenberg, will appear in subsequent issues of Psychoanalytic Dialogues. Although we have made a minimal attempt to critically review the different theories, we have allowed ourselves the fiction of trying to produce a representational text in which the different arguments of the different theorists are presented, more or less, in their own terms. While this version of textual production on our part may be troubling, as it obscures as much as it reveals, insofar as our own position is never quite

We wish to thank Victor Bonfilio, Sam Gerson, and Charles Spezzano for comments on various parts of this paper. Needless to say, we are responsible for all its faults and foibles.

For the purposes of clarity and stylistic consistency it was decided that Gerhardt's academic writing style fits the needs of this paper.

5

declared, our intent is to try to minimize our own mediating voice and focus on the different theorists in their own right by giving clinical examples to demonstrate their claims. The irony, even folly, of attempting to eliminate our own presence from this series on intersubjectivity is not lost on us—and neither is our plea for special circumstances. However, given the growing interest in the intersubjective turn, in order that it not be construed as privileging the emotional authenticity of the two-person exchange, we believe it is absolutely essential to understand the theorists' self-articulated arguments and to keep alive their differences rather than to assimilate the intersubjective perspective as a unified or hegemonic approach. It is in the spirit of keeping alive these differences—itself a crucial commitment of the intersubjective approach—that our somewhat exegetical text should be understood.

Dreams

Commentary of Paper by Hazel Ipp

Steven Ellman PHD

Steven Ellman

140 Riverside Drive

cellman 174@aol.com

Steven Ellman   Professor of Psychology, Doctoral Program in Clinic Psychology, City University of New York; Training Analyst (Fellow), Institute of Psychoanalytic Training and Research (IPTAR); and Faculty Member and Supervisor, Postdoctoral Program in Psychoanalysis, New York University. He is the author or coauthor of several books.

It is somewhat awkward to admit that, although i have spent a reasonable amount of my career involved in dream research (Ellman and Antrobus, 1991), I have never written a clinical paper on dreams. This undoubtedly has to do with a conflict that is a constant background presence in my clinical work—whether I am interested in the dream or the dreamer. In other words, is my interest in dreams moving the patient to dream in particular ways to satisfy the demand characteristics of my analytic stance? Of course, Freud faced this question when he hypothesized that dreams were the royal road to the unconscious. Subsequent clinical experience led him to realize that royal roads were rapidly proving to be dead ends in Europe and in psychoanalysis. It was his intellectual discovery of the importance of transference (and, implicitly, countertransference) that influenced him to abandon his reliance on dream interpretation and state that, although he did not doubt the importance of the dream, he realized that to rely fully on dream interpretation will put a clinician “into conflict with the most immediate aims of the treatment” (Freud, 1911, quoted in Ellman, 1991, p. 110.

responseSet.title: The Clinton Impeachment: Sexuality and Power Need Different Psychoanalytic Theories
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Clinton Impeachment

Sexuality and Power Need Different Psychoanalytic Theories

Isidoro Berenstein MD

Isidoro Berenstein

Republica de la India 2921, Apt. 9
(1425) Buenos Aires
Argentina
Email: iberens@intramed.net.ar

Isidoro Berenstein   Training and Supervising Analyst, Buenos Aires Psychoanalytic Association; winner of the Mary Sigourney Award 1993 for outstanding contribution to psychoanalysis; and Scientific Chair of the Department of Family Relations, Argentine Group Psychology and Psychotherapy Association. The editors thank Ernesto Mujica, Ph.D. for additional assistance in translation.

Psychoanalysts obtain the empirical basis for interpretation from the patient's material in the course of the analytic session. That is the arena for observing and analyzing the clinical facts. President Clinton is not our patient, but he set in motion a number of events that reached us as social facts. I begin with the Clinton case in order to explore the structure of power in relationships. I suggest that we live simultaneously in three different worlds that produce three kinds of facts—individual, linking, and social. Individual facts are those that we arbitrarily say happen inside the subject, in the inner world. Linking facts are those that happen in the space “between” two or more subjects, and within this category kinship relationships should be differentiated. What is special about these relationships is that they take place with a specific, differentiated other. I refer to the “other” as a subject of desire that exceeds the internal object that is projected onto it. In this sense, it is not simply an object of drive derivative or love object of the ego. Social facts are those that reach us from the social milieu formed by the undifferentiated others, defensively personified in particular characters who represent the world of politics, religion, finance, social class. These three worlds leave marks on the self that may or may not become unconscious inscriptions. It is desirable to consider that each mark has a particular and different origin. Only a part of the meaning of each mark is liable to be transferred from one world to the other. The clinical material of an individual patient, of a couple, or of a family as a patient does not have an equivalent in the social field.

291

The author comments on Dean and Dyess's “Gender: The Impossibility of Meaning.” They provide a valuable critique, based on Lacan's work, of postmodern gender theories. However, Dean and Dyess's argument for the “impossibility of meaning” in regard to gender may erect a new false dichotomy (gender as endless possibility versus gender as total impossibility) in place of the problematic dichotomy (gender essentialism versus gender constructivism) they so usefully deconstruct.

[[RunningHead]]

The Growing Convergence of Contemporary Psychoanalysis and Jungian Analysis

Mario Jacoby PHD

Mario Jacoby

Witellikerstrasse 4
CH-8702 Zollikon
Switzerland
mario.jacoby@bluewin.ch

Mario Jacoby   Lecturer and Training and Supervisory Analyst at the C. G. Jung Institute, Zurich; an analyst in private practice; and author of numerous articles and six books on analytical psychology.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

After his Break with Freud in 1913 Jung Discarded Many of the “golden rules” of psychoanalysis. Instead of using the couch in order to render himself “invisible,” he had his patients sit in an armchair facing the analyst. He abandoned the basic psychoanalytic rule of free association and tried to facilitate a dialogue in which the patient's problems and “the manifestations of the unconscious” could be discussed. Instead of listening with free-floating attention, he would spontaneously express his ideas about dreams and other material. He did not restrict himself to interpretation, but allowed himself, as the therapist, to come forth with his own emotional reactions.

What are the reasons to justify these modifications? First, they came out of Jung's growing experience of the mutual influence of analyst and analysand. “By no device can the treatment be anything but the product of mutual influence, in which the whole being of the doctor as well as that of his patient plays its part” (Jung, 1929, p. 71). Jung was a pioneer with this insight, which anticipated such contemporary models of therapy as the interactive field, systems analysis, and so forth.

Furthermore, Jung began to look at “all theories of neurosis and methods of treatment as a dubious affair”—arguing that any method of treatment can be determined only “by the nature of the case” (Jung, 1926, p. 113).

responseSet.title: The Erotic Leader
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Erotic Leader

Anderw Samuels D.H.L.

Anderw Samuels

148 Mercers Road
London N19 4PX
England
andrew.samuels@virgin.net

Anderw Samuels   Professor of Analytical Psychology, University of Essex; a Jungian training analyst, lecturer, and political consultant; and the author of The Political Psyche. His new book, The Secret Life of Politics, will be published in 2000.

In a nutshell, my proposal is that the strong collective and individual reactions to the trials and tribulations of President Clinton, leading to his survival in office, show that he has functioned in a good-enough way as an “erotic leader.”

From my point of view, a truly erotic leader is not a leader who simply hops in and out of bed with people. Clinton may actually be an inferior, degenerated, partial version of an erotic leader. But public obsession in many countries with the sex lives of leaders suggests that there is something about the actual, physical, corporeal eroticism of leadership that is worth exploring further. The concern of the media with the sexuality of leading figures in society generally, not just political leaders, suggests that, as in so many cases, mass consciousness has discovered something of interest. I think that the populace, in its obsession with the sexuality of leaders, is on to something important.

I see the good-enough erotic leader as using her or his sexuality to convey to citizens that they are exciting, creative, and autonomous people who can work together cooperatively. Ordinary admiration and warmth are extremely potent parts of psychological evolution and the establishment of self-esteem. Whether the leader communicates, in an almost physically warm way, that the leader admires the people is an interesting and neglected aspect of the political process.

My model for this notion is the work I have done on parenting and on clinical process—work focusing on what I have called erotic playback. In the family, this is the way in which the parent communicates to children of both sexes that they are admirable, physically desirable, and erotically viable creatures. Of course, in a family or in an analysis, I am referring only to incest fantasy and not to the physical enactment of such fantasy (which, by analogy, is what Clinton has done).

277

[[RunningHead]]

Dreaming to Different Beats

Reply to Commentaries

Hazel Ipp PHD

Hazel Ipp

183 St. Clair Avenue West
Toronto, Ontario M4V 1P7
Canada

Hazel Ipp  

It is both an honor and a challenge to respond to the commentaries on my clinical material. Each reflects considerable thought, effort, and respect. The different theoretical approaches produced considerable departures in terms of understanding and positioning of the dream material. Although I clearly found myself resonating to those approaches more closely allied with my own, I found each of the commentaries evocative in its own right—stirring new questions and generating fresh thoughts as I reconsidered Barbara along the different lines posited.

As always, working with disembodied clinical material is no easy task. So much of importance is lost in the telling, and those outside the analysis are not privy to the ambience; the to-and-fro over time; the naunces that blossom and take shape in the material and in the interactional field; and, most especially in this example, the extensions of meaning of the dream imagery and affect that are so often a continuation of much that has gone before in the analytic process itself. Given the small window opening into Barbara's world, Adams, Ellman, Fosshage, and Levenson have ably captured certain critical features that contribute to her essence.

The commentators' portrayls of Barbara are wide-ranging—she is, variously, seen as extremely narcissistic, prone to borderline rages, grandiose and with significant ego defects suggestive of a poor prognosis, a highly traumatized woman with an unrealistic sense of her own limitations who leads an indolent life with questionable values, and a woman with a growing sense of expansiveness and possibility as she renegotiates her sense of self and self with others through good use of the analytic process. Although there is some overlap in aspects of the understandings put forth, the spin put on them differs dramatically.

Afterword

A View from the Outside

James Fosshage PHD

James Fosshage

330 West 58th Street, Suite 200
New York, NY 10019
fosshage@psychoanalysis.net

James Fosshage  

For each psychoanalytic model, the motivation posited is fundamental and has a rippling effet throughout the theory and clinical application. Jung's theory is no different. What is different is that Jung was the first to posit a fundamental striving toward realizing the potentialities of the person, what Jung called self-realization and the process of individuation. Although conscious intentions exist, Jung posited that there is an unconscious center of the psyche, called the Self, that regulates and organizes “processes of maturation” (Jacoby). Although Young-Eisendrath refers to these psychological functions as the archetype of the self (not Self), and Sedgwick tends to be more concerned with the sense of self, all of our post-Jungians retain Jung's motivational model and the correspondent notion of unconscious self-regulatory and self-directing or maturational functions. Within psychoanalysis, this self-realization motivational model and view of the self, including self-regulatory functions, most closely correspond with the work of Winnicott and Kohut, and, not surprisingly, they are the two authors the post-Jungians refer to most.

Addressing Jung's major contributions, the post-Jungians listed the concept of Self and the corresponding motivational model; the primacy of affect; the bipersonal, mutual influence field created by analyst and analysand; the concept of archetypes; the required deep involvement of the analyst in the treatment; the analyst's sharing of his or her emotional reactions; the importance of the transference and countertransference; a life-span model; a theory of complexes; and the importance of dreams as a more direct expression of the analysand's psychological situation. It is my view that Jung is not known by psychoanalysts for many of these contributions. What is striking is how fitting many of these ideas are to a contemporary relational view of the analytic relationship. Indeed, Young-Eisendrath argues that “analytical psychology is consonant with postmodernism when its models and methods are understood from a nonessentialist viewpoint that is …not a revision but an extension of Jung's own thinking.

This reply clarifies the ideas originaly presented in “Beyond Milk and the Good Breast: Reconfiguring Psychoanalytic Dyads” (PD 9/5, 1999) in response to Steven Reisner's commentary. It faults Dr. Reisner's reading of Lacan and Kristeva, as well as his use of male-gendered metaphors to transform my clinical material into a different analytic treatment. I conclude that, by arriving at such differing conclusions regarding theory and clinical treatment, he inadvertently proves, and enacts, the basis of my argument: that the gender of the analyst is an important variable that affects psychoanalytic formulations, treatment, and outcome.

[[RunningHead]]

Sex, Lies, and Audiotape

Psychoanalysts Reflect on President Clinton's Impeachment

Emanuel Berman PHD and Jay Frankel PHD

Jay Frankel

Haifa 31905, Israel
Emanuel@psy.HAIFA.AC.IL

286 Tillou Road
South Orange, NJ 07079
Drifrankel@aol.com

Jay Frankel   Co-Director, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis; Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; and Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Are psychoanalysts capable of contributing, through their unique expertise, to the understanding of social and political topics? If so, should they get involved?

The dangers of such adventures far away from the consulting room have become clearer in recent decades. “Applied analysis,” as such nonclinical explorations used to be called, often became synonymous with omniscient reductionistic interpretations that disregarded the unique characteristics of their subject matter, be it literature, film (Berman, 1998), or politics, and turned everybody (national leaders, painters, fictional figures) into patients on our couch. Such imperialistic invasions often aroused the justified wrath of academic experts and of creative artists in the various fields invaded (Berman, 1997).

Should these unhappy results lead us to abandon any such endeavor? Not necessarily. Such a puristic solution may risk throwing the baby out with the bathwater.

In the past, a contrast was often suggested between the safe ground of clinical psychoanalysis, in which analysts can reach definitive understanding of the material, and the shaky ground of applied analysis, in which our efforts are more speculative. Our current perspective does not justify such a dichotomy.

267

Neil Altman PHD

Neil Altman

127 West 79th Street, Suite 3
New York, NY 10024
neilaltman@hotmail.com

Neil Altman  

I am very grateful to Drs. Gump and Cushman for their thoughtful, thought-provoking, and stimulating discussions of my paper.

When I first presented the paper, in Washington, DC, Gump was the discussant. She said, as you have now read, that Mr. A was “enlisting” me in his “struggle with a conflicted sense of self”—between his sense of self as a responsible professional man who could be expected to handle a checking account and his sense of self as a Bronx street tough who couldn't. I realized that, by accepting his initial offer to bring cash, I was communicating to him that I was unreflectively seeing him as the “street kid.”

This was a startling insight. It was one of those appalling and fascinating psychoanalytic moments in which one sees one's own blind spot. I realized that a racist attitude (in the form of low expectations) had been most evident in a place where I wasn't at all aware of it, for all my preoccupation with racism at many points in the treatment.

Where I might differ with Gump is in her comment that Mr. A. was seeking to evoke my “acceptance.” I think what is at issue in this as in all enactments is more complex. I believe that Mr. A was unconsciously seeking my disapproval, anger, and greed, as well as my acceptance and understanding. In my view, he was unconsciously seeking to put on the table between us the most problematic affects and interpersonal entanglements taking shape at the intersection of our psychic worlds, in this particular historical context. How he and I, both of us also seeking love and understanding, would find our way through this particular minefield was the question of the treatment.

We, as psychoanalysts, have been garnering considerable experience in managing such complexities, but we have been slow to apply our knowledge of the subtle and complex workings of the mind to the field of race relations on both individual and social levels. Our failure to make this application is revealing about the anxiety, shame, and guilt that attend the matter of race for us as Americans and as psychoanalysts.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

The Psyche as a Process

Beverley Zabriskie C.S.W.

Beverley Zabriskie

15412 East 74th Street
New York, NY 10021

Beverley Zabriskie   Jungian analyst in New York City, is Faculty, New York C. G. Jung Institute, and C. G. Jung Center of Mexico City, and Assistant Editor, Journal of Analytical Psychology and Journal of Jungian Theory and Practice.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Jung (1947) Perceived the Psyche as a Dynamic Interactive System that attempts to organize itself through “the equilibration of all kinds of opposites” (1947/1954, p. 207). The healthy psyche is an ongoing process between conscious and unconscious as two fields, a term and concept Jung adopted from William James. As two modes of psychic knowing and experiencing that relativize each other, these move along a vector toward individuation, understood as psychic balance. Via the interplay between these psychic complementarities, another term from James, psychic health is achieved through alternating rhythms of integration and disassociation. The practitioner is to enter into, attend, and participate in the self-reflective process by which a patient recognizes and negotiates the rhythms of opposites.

As a young psychiatrist working under Bleuler with an inpatient population, Jung perceived neurotic dissociation and more florid splitting as indications of an ego incapable of holding itself sufficiently intact to metabolize these tensions in the face of outer and inner challenges. This perspective emphasized the disassociative nature of symptoms, which informs much clinical work today.

In Jung's view, although symptoms emerge from unmetabolized residues of past experience, as psychic energy is also prospective (1928), they are also attempts from the unconscious to break through binding constructs of a fixated ego. Seemingly meaningless, each symptom may be unpacked for the meaning of both its contents and dynamic purpose.

Jung also approached the psyche as “a multiplicity within a unity,” based on his theory of complexes.

Adolf Grünbaum  

The psychoanalytic enterprise—especially the various modes of treatment designated “psychoanalytic therapy”—is now in crisis, if only because of the significant decline in the number of patients seeking such treatment. Therefore, it is understandable that some practitioners seek to protect their livelihoods by attempting to discredit criticism, if not the personal integrity of the critics.

From a simply human point of view, I feel some empathy with analysts who trained for years and paid for a very costly personal analysis, only to find that their earning a professional living is in some jeopardy. I suggested as much in my 1993 book (p. xi).

In his Introduction to the “Symposium on the Grünbaum Debate,” Jacobson (1996) offers an excuse for not having invited me to respond to my five critics (p. 500). In Jacobson's view, the post-Freudians achieve immunity against the gravamen of my objections to Freud. That they fail to do so is resoundingly demonstrated by the penetrating writings of Eagle (1983, 1993). Although Schwartz cites Eagle (1983), and Jacobson cites my 1993 book, Jacobson turns a blind eye to my book's prefatory statement about Eagle's pertinent (1983) demonstration: “Eagle … has contended in detail that the upshot of my assessment of Freud's classical arguments applies no less to the neorevisionist doctrines of [Kohutian] self-psychology, and of recent object relations theory” (Grünbaum, 1993, p. xii), a statement on which I elaborated (pp. 37-38). Eagle (1993) strengthens his telling conclusions even more.

Adolf Grünbaum is replying in particular to a discussion by Joseph Schwartz (1996) in Psychoanalytic Dialogues (6/4).Adolf Grünbaum, Ph.D. is the Andrew Mellon Professor of Philosophy of Science, Research Professor of Psychiatry, and Chairman of the Center for Philosophy of Science, University of Pittsburgh. He is the author of two critical books on psychoanalysis, among other books.335

The Clinton Impeachment

A View from France

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel PHD

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel

Paris 75007
France

Janine Chasseguet-Smirgel   Member, Paris Psychoanalytic Society, and Honorary Member, Philadelphia Psychoanalytic Society. She is known for her pioneering study on Female Sexuality (1964) outside the main Freudian stream and for her work on perversion (Creativity and Perversion, 1985).

For the french, the trial of the president of the united states was from beginning to end incredible, unimaginable, surrealistic. Your run-of-the-mill French citizen found the Americans to be both ridiculous, prudish, and feverishly preoccupied with smut. From the time of de Gaulle and the once-powerful local communists, we have repeatedly heard that Americans are naïve, backward, arrogant, and imperialistic. And now, voilà, here comes the trial providing made-to-order confirmation for these hand-me-down, rudimentary judgments. Imperialism and arrogance manifested themselves in the unbelievable indifference with respect to international opinion on the part of those who wanted this trial and who insisted on broadcasting, over Internet and television channels throughout the world, the rather pitiful pornography of the Starr report and the degrading images of the world's most powerful man with his pants down. The America that was thus revealed could only cause its enemies to rejoice—they could not have hoped or wished for more. As to the view of the backward character of Americans, it was immediately thrust to the forefront by intellectuals who spoke of the Inquisition, witch hunts, McCarthyism, an inconceivable invasion of privacy, and a blow to individual freedom. There was a communion of the traditional, mainstream right-and left-wing parties in a unanimous condemnation of the proceedings. Newspapers ran “America Has Gone Amok” headlines, and magazine covers showed Clinton crucified on a feminine pubis, transposing in this way the poster of the film The People vs. Larry Flint. A well-known feminist, Elisabeth Badinter, wrote an article in the Nouvel Observateur (1998)

Relational Trouble

Reply to Commentaries

Cynthia Dyess MD and Tim Dean PHD

Tim Dean

1505 11th Avenue, Suite 306
Seattle, WA 98122
cdyess@connectexpress.com

Urbana, IL 61801
dean@uiuc.edu

Tim Dean  

This reply to the commentaries by Corbett, Hansell, and Stern explores whether Lacan's concept of the real can—or should—be translated into more readily recognizable terms. It extends our previous discussion of impossibility by arguing that not all ideas and experiences can be brought within the realm of the known and familiar. We suggest that impossibilities of meaning should not be understood primarily in phenomenological terms, and we demur from the assessment that our concept of impossibility offers nothing for clinical work. Claiming that what resists meaning also impedes relationality, we encourage relational theorists to address the nonrelational processes that subtend relationality, including the relation between analyst and patient. We acknowledge that the theory of impossibility—or what we now call “negative mediation”—raises a fundamental challenge to relational theory, but we insist that disruptions of relationality need not be considered pathological. Taking into account the nonrelational may enhance rather than impoverish relational psychoanalysis.

responseSet.title: A Gift in Return: The Clinical Use of Writing About a Patient
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

A Gift in Return

The Clinical Use of Writing About a Patient

Stuart Pizer ABPP

Stuart Pizer

152 Brattle Street
Cambridge, MA 02138
SAPIZER@psychoanalysis.net

Stuart Pizer   President-elect, Supervising Analyst and Faculty, Masschusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Instructor in Psychology, Harvard Medical School; Founding Chair of the Ethics Committee of the Psychoanalytic Consortium; and a Contributing Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

I have titled this paper “A Gift in Return” because I consider it to be a gift to the analyst when a patient grants permission for clinical material to be used in writing, presenting, and publishing, and because those instances in which the patient actually benefits from the analyst's writing strike me as an incidental repayment of the kindness.

I do not intend my message in this paper to be “Go forth and write about all your patients because it will do them good.” It may or may not. Writing about one's patients is a project full of responsibilities and fraught with risks. We cannot and ought not rely on any all-purpose assumptions about the positive or negative effects on our patients when we write about our work with them. I have chosen to relate two treatments in which the patients benefited dramatically from being written about because I thought it would be of interest to share that such things have occurred in practice. First, though, I review some of the hazards and problematics that must be kept in mind when we undertake to make use of clinical material in our writing.

First, we must remain sensitive to potential misuses of the power asymmetry in the analytic relationship. As we weight the choice of writing about a particular patient, at a particular point during or after the treatment, we need to juxtapose the relative benefits of reporting the clinical data to our colleagues with the relative costs to the patient or the therapeutic process.

An earlier version of this paper was presented as an Invited Address to the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) at the annual meeting of the American Psychological Association, San Francisco, August 1998.

Self and Transcendence

A Postmodern Approach to Analytical Psychology in Practice

Polly Young-Eisendrath PHD

Polly Young-Eisendrath

166 Battery Street
Burlington, VT 05401
pye@together.net

Polly Young-Eisendrath   Jungian analyst and psychologist practicing in Burlington, Vermont, where she is also Clinical Associate Professor of Psychiatry at the University of Vermont Medical College. She is a member of the New York Association for Analytic Psychology.

In the foreground of jung's contributions to psychodynamic theory is the idea that the unity of personality is a struggle both in the moment and over time. Although everyone strives for a coherent and continuous sense of self, this state of being is not easily sustained or ever finally secured. According to Jung, personality is structured as multiple centers of subjectivity, loosely organized around the ego complex with its core of the archetype of self. These “psychological complexes” are experienced as motivating forces with core affective and image components that are highly arousing.

In the following account, I show how Jung's theory of psychological complexes fits with contemporary research on emotions and emotional memory and how his dissociative model of personality is balanced by a theory of integration and unity through the archetype of self. Additionally, I argue that analytical psychology is consonant with postmodernism when its models and methods are understood from a nonessentialist viewpoint that is, in my view, not a revision but an extension of Jung's own thinking.

Archetype and Complex

responseSet.title: Bill Clinton and John F. Kennedy: The Dark Side of Charisma
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Bill Clinton and John F. Kennedy

The Dark Side of Charisma

Sue Erikson Bloland C.S.W.

Sue Erikson Bloland

180 Sullivan Street
New York, NY 10012
sbloland@bway.net

Sue Erikson Bloland   Faculty, Manhattan Institute for Psychoanalysis, and Co-Director, Center for the Study of Mid-Life Development, New York City.

Bill clinton and john f. kennedy are both exceptionally charismatic figures who achieved enormous popularity as presidents. And both have been revealed (one posthumously and one while still in office) as compulsive womanizers who risked their political careers (if not the welfare of the country) in the addictive pursuit of sexual gratification. Should we be shocked to learn that these well-loved leaders—or that Mao Ze-dong, Gandhi, or Martin Luther King—qualify as sexual addicts?

What we have come to know posthumously about the personal conduct of many of history's most extraordinary political leaders suggests that a combination of charismatic personality, a drive to attain power, and sexual compulsivity is a common pattern among heads of state. How might we understand the psychological connection between sexual addiction, charisma, and the quest for power? In his biography of Kennedy, Hersh (1997) described how little Jack was hospitalized with scarlet fever at the age of two and was then sent, by himself, to a sanatorium for three months to recuperate. It was there, Hersh asserted, that “Jack, torn from his parents and left in the care of strangers, demonstrated the first signs of what would be a lifelong ability to attract attention by charming others. He so captivated his nurse that it was reported that she begged to be allowed to stay with him” (p. 14). This is to me a vivid description of the birth of charisma in a personality so compelling that it eventually charmed and captivated people the world over.

We tend to think of charisma as a magical power or gift to win the devotion of others. The charismatic personality gives an appearance of exceptional self-confidence, of strength and authority, which inspires trust, confidence, and adulation in others.

285

Consultation from the Consultant's Perspective

Glen Gabbard MD

Glen Gabbard

Box 829
Topeka, KS 66601-0829
gabbargo@menninger.edu

Glen Gabbard   Callaway Distinguished Professor of Psychoanalysis, Menninger Clinic; Director and Training and Supervising Analyst, Topeka Institute for Psychoanlysis; author of Love and Hate in the Analytic Setting; and coauthor (with Eva Lester) of Boundaries and Boundary Violations in Psychoanalysis.

In this paper, i address a subset of my experiences as a consultant to other analysts and therapists. Specifically, I discuss my work with clinicians who are engaged in transgressions of boundaries that are serious and potentially harmful to their patients. In such situations, the consultant inevitably assumes certain superego functions in trying to stop a perilous situation from escalating. I wish to stress, however, that in the less extreme and more usual situations, the consultant should optimally avoid a critical or disapproving stance lest it interfere with the consultee's capacity to openly share details of the clinical process.

There is a fundamental paradox in the psychoanalytic training system. For a period of several years, analytic candidates learn the psychoanalytic method through weekly supervision meetings in which they present process notes in painstaking detail to a supervisor who assists them in understanding the intrapsychic and interpersonal processes inherent in the work. In most psychoanalytic institutes, a candidate cannot graduate until he or she has worked in supervision with three or four different supervising analysts. Hence, the analytic identity is forged not in a two-person situation with the patient but in a three-person situation that includes a supervisor.

At some point, the candidate graduates. What ensues can only be described as peculiar.

An earlier version of this paper was presented as an Invited Address to the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) at the annual meeting of the American Psychological Association, San Francisco, August 1998.

Haunted Talk, Healing Action

Commentary on Paper by Kimberlyn Leary

Adrienne Harris PHD

Adrienne Harris

5th Floor
New York, NY 10003
aharris@psychoanalysis.net

Adrienne Harris   Faculty Member and Supervisor, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis, and an Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

Kimberlyn leary brings together several distinct areas of psychoanalytic theorizing in order to illuminate the particular knot that race occupies in conscious and unconscious psychic and social life. She interweaves considerations of transference and countertransference as scenes of enactments and analyzes the interpenetration of history and psyche, the transgenerational transmission of trauma, and the lived psychic consequences. Although Leary's focus is the manifestation and impact of “the racialized subjectivity” in clinical experience, she raises interesting questions about technique and ways of working with and writing about enactments more generally.

In Leary's hands, analysis of the effects and meanings of race makes for a complex, multiply configured constellation of psychic, historical, and material factors. In one of the strongest images in her essay, race becomes a transitional space, both a vehicle for psychodynamics and a complex, layered space in which depth and surface are never fully what they seem and in which racialized subjectivity is made and created. In that space, one must attend to the intersection of race with differences of class and of privilege, the particular arenas in which race and gender cohere and fracture, the frighteningly seamless enmeshment of constructions of race in silence, in procedural memory, in the unconscious.

Leary stresses the fragility of the conversation around race outside and inside the consulting room, its vulnerability to narcissistic disturbance and disequilibrium.

655

Randall Sorenson PHD PSYD

Randall Sorenson   Faculty, Training, and Supervising Analyst at the Institute of Contemporary Psychoanalysis in Los Angeles and Associate Professor of psychology at Rosemead School of Psychology in La Mirada, California. He is in private practice in Pasadena, California.

Psychoanalysts have likened psychoanalytic institutes to religious denominations and have viewed any possible similarities as stubborn but temporary liabilities that should eventually be overcome. Theoretical and empirical studies in the sociology of religious denominations suggest otherwise and offer a different way to think about institute structures. If a sociology for other kinds of groups applies to psychoanalytic ones, there are important implications for how we think to structure psychoanalytic education.

The aim of this paper is to discuss racial enactments for what they might contribute to our understanding of the intersubjectivity of race and racial experience. “Racial enactments” designate interactive sequences embodying the actualization in the clinical situation of cultural attitudes toward race and racial difference. I present examples of racial enactments in several social contexts, as well as in an extended clinical vignette. I consider racial enactments in the light of contemporary psychoanalytic theory and suggest that collaborative methods facilitate the effective analysis of racial material.

Commentary on Paper by Gerhardt, Sweetnam, and Borton

Jessica Benjamin PHD

Jessica Benjamin

Jessica Benjamin   Jessica Benjamin, Ph. D. is on the faculties of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and the Psychoanalytic Studies Program of the New School for Social Research. Her most recent book is Shadow of the Other.

I want to start by expressing my appreciation for the deeply thoughtful discussion of my work provided by Gerhardt, Sweetnam, and Borton. It is gratifying to see my ideas taken up in their project of formulating the intersubjective turn in psychoanalysis, even though at times I will want to question these formulations or approach them from a slightly different angle. As these authors have not only sought to synthesize my theoretical contribution but also to supply clinical applications, they have offered me a welcome opportunity to respond to a discussion of practical issues. I am particularly grateful for this opportunity to rethink and clarify some of my key concepts in relation to these clinical applications.

It may be relevant to begin with a word about the experience of listening to a rendering of my own ideas by an “other.” It is a mixed experience to hear another person's reception of one's ideas, even when the reception is basically recognizing and affirming, because the ideas—an expression of my subjectivity—are returned transformed, no longer identical to what they once were, now amalgamated with the other's subjectivity, partly other. Ogden (1996) takes precisely this starting point in the introduction to his elegant disquisition on intersubjectivity, telling the reader that she or he will make something different of this book, something that is the reader's own.

I think this experience illustrates something elusive about the relation between negation and recognition.

43

This paper begins with an analysis of race as a social construction and then follows the argument that, at a deep structural level, race and racism are organized by the same rational-irrational polarity of Enlightenment philosophy that informs psychoanalytic structural theory. The heart of the paper is formed by two case examples, one from my own practice and one from Leary (1997). I argue that unconscious racism is to be expected in our clinical work at this point in history and that truly reparative efforts depend on an acknowledgement of racism in the transference-countertransference matrix.

Thomas Ogden   Co-Director, Center for the Advanced Study of the Psychoses, San Francisco; Supervising and Training Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute

In this paper, I explore “the art of mourning” in the course of discussing two Borges prose poems, “Pierre Menard, Author of the Quixote” (1941) and “Borges and I” (1957), both of which were written soon after Borges suffered enormous emotional losses. I suggest that successful mourning centrally involves a demand that we make on ourselves to create something—whether it be a memory, a dream, a story, a poem, a response to a poem—that begins to meet, to be equal to, the full complexity of our relationship to what has been lost and to the experience of loss itself. Paradoxically, in this process, we are enlivened by the experience of loss and death, even when what is given up or is taken from us is an aspect of ourselves.

Making a Memorial Place

The Photography of Shimon Attie

Jeanne Bernstein PHD

Jeanne Bernstein

1808 B Fourth Street
Berkeley, CA 94710

Jeanne Bernstein   Faculty, Personal and Supervising Analyst at the Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, San Francisco and on the adjunct faculty at The Wright Institute, Berkeley.

Assisting the analysand in making the past a living present held within a bearable yet unpredictable future is what psychoanalysis is so much about. Events of the past can never be redone, or fully repossessed, but they must be reconstructed in the context of the transference-countertransference experience of the analytic relationship. In order to make the past a useful present and presence, we need metaphors to give it shape—metaphors that capture the memorial activity linking the past with the present and future in a meaningful manner. In this paper, I explore the ways in which the work of the American photographer Shimon Attie creates a memorial place in which the past is not simply remembered but instead is actively mourned. In The Writing on the Wall, Attie collects broken fragments from prewar German-Jewish life in Berlin, and, by projecting these found shards of former lives onto the buildings in the Berlin Scheunenviertel, which once housed these people, he creates a potential space in which a present can suddenly come alive by the superimposition of a past that was supposed to have been obliterated. In this transitional realm, the spectator is given a wide realm of to-and-fro movements between past and present that permits the creation of an object world that did not exist before. I suggest that Attie's intricate weavings of past, present, and future serve as instructive models for the psychoanalytic process in which the analyst can find himself in a similar position of opening up a playground where the past

I express my profound appreciation to Thomas H. Ogden for his generous and encouraging help with this paper. I thank Shelley Nathans and Sam Gerson for introducing me to the photography of Shimon Attie. Without Zeese Papanikolas's strong encouragement, I would never have written this paper. In addition, I thank most sincerely Michael A. Bernstein for teaching me so much about the Shoa. Last, I thank Shimon Attie for having created such a powerful and inspiring series of photographs (The Writing on the Wall) and for providing me with the slides of his work, courtesy of the Jack Shaiman Gallery in New York City.

347

can be brought into the present and where the presentness of the past can come alive. The ability to move back and forth between the present and the past suggests a link to the Freudian concept of deferred action, later taken up by Lacan under the notion of après-coup, where the impression (Prägung) of an earlier event, having lain dormant for a long time, breaks through into the present through a retroactive action that then completely reshapes the present impression. I draw comparisons to psychoanalytic practice, in which the superimposition (the stacking on top of one another) of recollections, dreams, and associations pries open in the analysand's mind a psychic space in which memories of a seemingly insignificant past absorb a sudden sense of urgency when revisited through this retroactive process.

When knowledge comes, memory comes too, little by little. Knowledge and memory are one and the same thing.

Saul Friedlander

Already a fictitious past occupies in our memories the place of another, a past of which we know nothing with certainty, not even that it is false.

Jorge Luis Borges

Irwin Hoffman   Faculty Member and Supervising Analyst at the Chicago Center for Psychoanalysis and a Lecturer in Psychiatry at the University of Illinois College of Medicine. He is on the editorial boards of The International Journal of Psychoanalysis and Psychoanalytic Dialogues and is an Editorial Reader for The Psychoanalytic Quarterly. This paper explores the interrelationship between patients' exercise of will to make advances in an analysis and their readiness to forgive their analysts for their human limitations. There is a thin line between idealization of the analyst, probably a necessary component of the process, and resentment of the analyst for his or her privilged position in the world and in the analytic situation itself. The patient's “progress” emerges as a kind of reparative gift, one that implicitly overcomes the patient's tendency to withhold such change out a sense of chronic, malignant envy. Particularly poignant in terms of its potential to elicit the patient's reparative concern is the situation in which the analyst is struggling with his or her mortality because of aging or life-threatening illness. In this essay two clinical vignettes are presented to illustrate some of the issues that this situation poses. One begins with an elderly patient appearing at the door of the analyst's (the author's) home the day of his return from the hospital after coronary bypass surgery. The other begins with an analyst who is terminally ill appearing at the door of a patient who is threatening suicide. The two stories are compared in terms of their implications for human agency, the exercise of will, and the coconstruction of meaning in the face of mortality in the analytic process.

Lewis Aron   Director of the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis and an Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

An essential principle of psychoanalytic practice is the maintenance of strict confidentiality, and yet the presentation and publication of psychoanalytic case histories necessitate considerable public disclosure of the lives of our patients. Inasmuch as psychoanalysis is a particularly frequent, intensive, and lengthy process, a report of the unfolding of an analysis necessarily entails considerable revelation concerning patients, their inner worlds, and their life circumstances. This use of confidential material raises innumerable ethical concerns, and psychoanalysis, with its unique emphasis on unconscious mental processes, also adds to the complexity of ethical considerations by demanding that we take unconcsious factors into account. When we speak, for example, of “informed consent” as an ethical principle, we as psychoanalytic clinicians must grapple with the problem of whether to take a patient's manifest acquiescence at face value. This article explores such ethical considerations along with other ethical and clinical complications in the presentation of analytic material for professional purposes.

This essay, a commentary on Sorenson's “Psychoanalytic Institutes as Religious Denominations,” explores a number of questions concerning the fate of psychoanalysis and the institutions that support it. How can psychoanalysis best survive its current travails, transcend the internecine conflicts that have always plagued it, and ride its contemporary reformation into a vital, relevant and creative second century? Stephen Mitchell

251 West 71st Street, #1B

Stephen Mitchell  

I am extremely grateful for the generosity displayed by these commentaries on my clinical vignette, my patient's dream, and our work with it. When the editors of this special project in comparative psychoanalysis asked if I would be willing to have this material used as a common point of reference, I remember a moment of trepidation. Psychoanalytic discourse is often partisan, belligerent, and sniping, and the author who presents actual clinical work regularly becomes a target. At least the presenter generally has had the last word, and, in the old days, when we believed that there was a “correct” and “exact” interpretation of clinical material, including dreams, the presenter, who knew the patient's history and life course way beyond the stark selectivity that any case presentation demands, could claim some sort of proprietary knowledge. He could then turn the tables on his ungracious commentators and assign them bad grades for being off the mark.

That this has so often been the norm makes the constructive tone of these commentaries even more impressive. Each author here has a sense that something important was going on in Andrew's treatment, in him and between us, and that we were both struggling to make the most out of his dream. But each commentator has his or her own take on this material—what he or she sees in it and would like to make out of it. Given the constructivist epistemology that makes the most sense to me these days, I certainly don't feel I have any privileged understanding of this dream and its meaning. What these different commentaries suggest to me is the deeply subjective, personal nature of each author's analytic vision as well as the common, shared (might we even use a revised sense of the term objective) understanding of what constitutes deep analytic work and transformation, whatever we might like to call it (relational, Freudian, Jungian, etc.).

Therefore, in response I would simply like to note the features of these different readings of the dream that seem particularly rich and distinctive to me—reflective, I assume, of each author's sensibilities and vision of what is most fundamental in life.

Ken Corbett PHD

Ken Corbett

3 East 10th Street
New York, NY 10003

Ken Corbett   Coeditor of Studies in Gender and Sexuality and a member of the Psychoanalytic Society of the New York University Postdoctoral Program.

By vigorously arguing for the utility of the lacanian real, dyess and dean seek to illustrate a theoretical scaffolding for that which cannot be emotionally understood. By locating gender in the real (following Copjec, 1994), they seek to position gender outside emotional understanding and/or communicative process. Aligned with the real, gender becomes an “inescapable failure of meaning,” a “stumbling block” that cannot be mastered either through reason or empathy, and as such subverts full relationality. If gender is the pulse of Dyess and Dean's essay, relationality is the heart; gendder serves as the “prime example” of their proposition not only that signification is limited but that full relationality is structurally impossible. As they explain in footnote 2, they pursue gender's limits in the service of their Lacanian project of “reimagin[ing] relationality—and hence community—in terms of impediment, impossibility, and failure.”

It seems likely that many analysts will readily recognize the impossibility of which Dyess and Dean speak—that illusory quality of gender, its inexplicable and ineradicable oscillation, its slippery silence, and at times its sheer lack of “it-ness.” At the same time, though, it seems equally likely that many analysts will not readily resonate with how Dyess and Dean conceive of gender's impossibility and in particular with how they conceive gender and relationality relative to the authority they grant to the real.

In part, I am proposing that this lack of resonance is a consequence of Dyess and Dean's employment of a way of thinking that is not familiar to most American analysts.

White Guilt, Political Activity, and the Analyst

Commentary on Paper by Neil Altman

Philip Cushman PHD

Philip Cushman

Oakland, CA 94618
pcushman@cspp.edu

Philip Cushman   Associate Professor, Core Faculty, California School of Professional Psychology, Alameda, and is in private practice in Oakland, CA.

I thank dr. altman for his willingness to discuss the difficult topic of race and ethnicity and to offer his work—and himself—as a subject of study. His article provides an opportunity to explore certain issues that I would like to discuss hermeneutically—that is, historically and politically—and by doing so advance values to which I know both of us subscribe.

Psychologists tend to over-psychologize the origins and effects of racial oppression. Mainstream psychologists usually understand prejudice or racism as a universal occurrence caused by natural processes or inherent structures of the mind activated by a putative universal human tendency to create in-groups and out-groups by generalizing about others and creating stereotypes. In their minds, the social realm enters the picture as only one “causative factor” among many. Although this academic tradition has had a long lineage and has served important functions in the ongoing attempt to secure a place for psychology in 20th-century U. S. society, lately it has been encountering opposition from some multiculturalists and from what is called the linguistic or interpretive turn (see Rabinow and Sullivan, 1987). How this debate plays out in psychoanalysis will constitute an important aspect of the movement's historical legacy. Altman's work is an important contribution to that debate.

The universal-feature argument, the position Allport (1954) popularized, is one much beloved by psychologists of most theoretical stripes, but its accuracy has recently been called into question. As Gaines and Reed (1996) pointed out, W. E. B. DuBois's (1903) position is much preferable to Allport's.

607

Termination of an “Infinite Conversation”

Reflections on the Last Days of an Analysis

Karol Marshall PHD

Karol Marshall

1425 Western Avenue, Suite 302
Seattle, WA 98101-2036
karol@connectexpress.com

Karol Marshall   Executive Director and Faculty, Northwest Center for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Assistant Professor, Department of Psychology, University of Washington; and psychoanalyst in Seattle.

I report on the conclusion of a distinctly nonmodernist analysis, one with no definitive destination or goal. Given the lack of case reports describing other such termination processes within the psychoanalytic tradition, I look outside the usual psychoanalytic literature for frameworks that might guide the experience of termination without directing or essentializing it. Psychoanalytic writing, like that of most disciplines, generally follows forms that strive for reasonableness, coherence, order, and meaning. I experiment with discussing the end of an analysis without organizing the narrative, its “truth,” images, subjects, and objects, according to more structured, scientific ways of thinking. Poststructural French literary figures Blanchot and Bataille as well as American philosopher Greene, aid my reflections on some of the mysteries mobilized in the analytic relationship and its ending.

The Therapeutic Action of Writing About Patients

Commentary on Papers by Lewis Aron and by Stuart A. Pizer

Samuel Gerson PHD

Samuel Gerson

2252 Fillmore St.
San Francisco, CA 94115
samgerson@aol.com

Samuel Gerson   Personal and Supervising Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California, and Professor, California School of Professional Psychology.

Going public with information developed in privacy is always an act brimming with complexity. The therapeutic and legal communities have both embraced the belief that individuals will be most forthright in disclosing information about themselves, and thereby enhancing their opportunity for benefit, if there is a guarantee that the information will not be used to harm the one who divulges it. This has been codified into laws protecting confidentiality and granting the help seeker the privilege of determining whether to permit a wider audience access to what is recognized as his or her own private property. Our ethical standards not only embody the legal but also implicitly elaborate the rationale that the act of going public with any information is so fraught with danger of harm that a provider who wishes to do so should first treat that wish with great suspicion. It is this assumption that I wish to highlight and question, as it is laced throughout the papers by Dr. Aron and Dr. Pizer. I wish to ask, in the simplest way, is the act of writing about a patient better thought of as helpful rather than harmful? Further, how might a psychoanalytic perspective illuminate this issue?

Aron's reminder that we as analysts must take into account unconscious motivations and meanings is an essential starting point. In a scholarly overview of the literature about the ethics of writing, Aron provides us with a clear summary of our concerns with this issue dating from our origins as a discipline.

[[RunningHead]]

A Plea for Sincere Lies

Slavoj Žižek PHD

Slavoj Žižek

Kotnikova 12
1000 Lubljana
Slovenia
Slavoj.zizek@guest.arnes.si

Slavoj Žižek   Senior Researcher, Department of Philosophy, University of Lubljana, Slovenia. His latest publications are The Plague of Fantasies, 1997 and The Ticklish Subject, 1999.

President Clinton's impeachment troubles call to mind the old, premodern identification of the health (or, rather, virility) of the king's body with the flourishing of his country, as exemplified in the Fisher King legend, in which the king's wound and the devastation of the landscape (the “wasteland”) are strictly correlated. Here it is also that the public display of the president's potency seems to have an uplifting effect on the “state of the union.” So what is effectively going on here?

There is an obvious Freudian background of Clinton's recent troubles: It is as if Clinton's sexual escapades resuscitated Freud's scenario of the primal father (Urvater) who possessed and sexually exploited all the women in his tribe and who was subsequently murdered by the coalition of his sons. After the parricide, the brothers established the rule of law, the civilized order based on the mutual acceptance of ethical prohibitions. So, will the resolution of the U. S. presidential crisis also consist of the fraternal pact of reciprocity—a “bipartisan agreement” between Republicans and Democrats to stop prying into each other's private lives—and thus of the birth of a new “civilization” out of the impeachment of the master?

This interesting theory nonetheless gives rise to at least two questions. In contrast to the mythical primal father's being free to rape and enjoy all women, Clinton's sexual behavior with Monica Lewinsky was marked by the acceptance of a fundamental prohibition—no sexual intercourse proper, no phallic penetration, even (perhaps) no overt active gestures on Clinton's part.

271

Standing in the spaces: essays on clinical process, trauma, and dissociation by Philip M. Bromberg. (Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1998, 328 pp.)

ONE OF MY FAVORITE PASSAGES IN THE COLLECTED WORKS OF Harry Stack Sullivan involves a visit with Sullivan's elderly father:

During my father's period of being a widower, I visited him. On this occasion, we sat and talked in the room which I had always liked best in the house. In the process, I noticed that the old gentleman was getting a bit distracted. In my usual fashion I went over the context of our conversation and found nothing at all offensive to him. And so, in the margin of my mind, I grew more and more puzzled over what was clear to me as some unsatisfactory mental state in my father. Finally he said, “Well, what do you think of the wallpaper?” On hearing this question, I was able to vividly recall that in an hour and a half's conversation, I had studied every damned line at which the wallpaper came together. I had not only observed that it was new wallpaper applied since my last visit, but I had made a minute study for any poor workmanship or regrettable defects in its application. But that had in no sense disturbed my consciousness.

531

Reply to Reviews by Cavell, Sorenson, and Smith

Philip Bromberg PHD

Philip Bromberg

300 Central Park West
New York, NY 10024

Philip Bromberg   Philip M. Bromberg, Ph. D. is a Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Psychoanalytic Institute and Clinical Professor of Psychology at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis. He is an Associate Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

According to Reik (1964), freud allegedly once said that “he could defend himself when attacked but was entirely defenseless when praised” (p. 4). Even though I've quoted this line before (Bromberg, 1997), I hadn't realized, until now, how much harder it is to be defenseless when the usual ways you've learned to hide your defenselessness when the usual ways you've learned to hide your defenselessness are denied to you. Therefore, as I have no choice but to reveal all, I might as well just begin with my strongest reaction—gratitude to my three distinguished colleagues, Drs. Marcia Cavell, Randall Sorenson, and Henry Smith, for not only their generous praise and all-around goodwill, but for their equal generosity in sharing, always with an inspiring blend of warmth and candor, the points of divergence with their own thinking. So, before replying to the individual reviews, I want to thank them all, collectively, for their thoughtful treatment of my work and for having dedicated so much of their valuable time to these commentaries. I am especially honored that my writing was taken as the object of such careful reading by individuals who, in their own right, have made such notable contributions to the field and whose own work I have followed with admiration and respect.

Although space does not permit my being able to express to each of them, in turn, the fullness of reply that is merited, I want to at least acknowledge how valuable it has been to reconsider my work from their fresh and diverse perspectives.

551

On the Desymbolization of Psychoanalytic Metaphors of Gender

Commentary on Velleda C. Ceccoli's “Beyond Milk and the Good Breast: Reconfiguring the Maternal Function in Psychoanalytic Dyads”*

Steven Reisner PHD

Steven Reisner

31 West 11th Street, #6C
New York, NY 10011
sreisner@psychoanalysis.net

Steven Reisner  

Ceccoli (1999a) argues that, because of their capacity for maternity, women analysts are capable of certain interventions that men are not. Taking issue with such assertions, this commentary argues that although the gendered metaphors of psychoanalytic intervention have changed usefully since Freud's paternalistic imagery, in favor of the maternal language of Klein and Winnicott, these metaphors are regressive if their value as symbolism is undermined. Ceccoli's case study is revisited and reevaluated to posit an alternative view: that theory is sometimes employed to fill gaps that might be more productively tolerated in the service of the analysis. It is argued that Ceccoli's use of Kristevan theory to support an essentialist position that translocates the paternal phallus into the female analysts' “gendered, bodily specificity … on the basis of our capacity for maternity” (p. 695) is an example of such a use of theory.

The analyst situates himself on a ridge where, on the one hand, the ‘maternal’ position—gratifying needs, ‘holding’ (Winnicott)

Velleda C. Ceccoli's paper appeared in Psychoanalytic Dialogues (9/5).

Steven Reisner, Ph. D. is a psychoanalyst in private practice and Faculty at the International Center for Trauma Studies at New York University. He teaches psychoanalytic theory in the Program in Clinical Psychology, Columbia University, Teachers College, and at the Women's Therapy Center Institute.

An earlier version of this discussion was presented as part of a panel, “When a Man Needs a Women,” at the spring 1997 meeting of the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) of the American Psychological Association, Denver, Colorado. I thank the editors of Psychoanalytic Dialogues for allowing the dialogue begun in that forum to continue in the pages of this journal.

Ceccoli and I both cite the same chapter, “Freud and Love: Treatment and its Discontent.” The citation dates differ because Ceccoli's source for that chapter is Tales of Love (1987) whereas mine is The Kristeva Reader (1983). The translations are the same.

—and on the other the ‘paternal’ position—the differentiation, distance and prohibition that produces both meaning and absurdity—are intermingled and severed, infinitely and without end [Kristeva, 1983, p. 246].
