[[RunningHead]]

Termination What Can Institutes Do?

Commentary on Paper by Randall Lehmann Sorenson

Kenneth Eisold PHD

Kenneth Eisold

353 Central Park West
New York, NY 10025
keneisold@aol.com

Kenneth Eisold   Director of the Organizational Program at the William Alanson White Institute, where he trains consultants in working psychodynamically with organizations. He is a practicing psychoanalyst as well as an organizational consultant, and he has written extensively on the psychodynamics of large systems. He is a Fellow of the A. K. Rice Institute.

Dr. Sorenson's paper includes an important warning about the future of analytic training: Like liberal, inclusive, and adaptive Protestant churches, analytic institutes, under the influence of contemporary pluralism, could lose not only their unique distinctiveness but the power to compel significant adherence and commitment on the part of their members. At the same time, I think, the paper includes a kind of tendentious and specious reasoning about the relationship between psychoanalysis and religion that poses a danger of a different kind.

“Is pluralism the kiss of death for psychoanalysis?” There is, I think Sorenson agrees, no compelling alternative to pluralism, unless you embrace a fanatical and sectarian school of thought closed off to influence from other points of view. Pluralism is dictated not only by psychoanalytic politics in this “postapostolic age” but by postpositivistic notions of the relationship between theory and practice. But does this mean that, eventually, all institutes will become indistinguishable, striving to teach essentially, the same blend of theories, or that training will become dumbed down to a smorgasbord of perspectives? Worse, will the more fanatical schools of thought—“full of passionate intensity,” in Yeats's phrase—drive out those of us who “lack all conviction”? Sorenson uses the example of the Independent School in Great Britain—losing ground to the Kleinians, whose candidate-analysands are increasing in number.

909

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Reply to Commentary

Kimberlyn Leary PHD

Kimberlyn Leary

527 East Liberty Street, Suite 209D
Ann Arbor, MI 48104
kimleary@umich.edu

Kimberlyn Leary  

It is indeed a formidable challenge to speak—and to listen— across racial lines. The need for such conversations is great, but, as we know, the stakes are high and thus the outcome is often less than certain. My experience of Dr. Harris's discussion is of feeling heard. Harris raises questions that are vital to the effort to expand our psychoanalytic understanding of prejudice and also of usable, effective racial identities.

My intention in presenting some of my work with Gloria was to capture some of the unmetabolized clinical process that goes on in everyday clinical practice when issues of race and identity come into play. I agree with Harris that it is in those exchanges that we may come to see, as she puts it, the “haunting presence” of encrypted racial trauma. It is that trauma that becomes enacted. Such moments show our gravitation to the racial reasoning that Toni Morrison (1992) and Cornel West (1999) suggested lies at the heart of cultural experience in the United States. Racial enactments show us our collective susceptibility to the cultural milieu in which we live and work.

Harris organizes my clinical material in terms of the escalation and oscillation of the shaming experience of envying and being envied. This is a quite useful perspective. Her comments bring out a critical dimension of the clinical process. They resonate with the way in which conventional “markers of privilege” (e.g., a college decal or admission to an analytic institute) are often understood to be, in some ineluctable sense, “white.” To the extent that Gloria and I buy into this perspective (one strand of the racial enactment), our thinking about each other and ourselves does become impoverished. The ensuing racial enactments become the location of our differing efforts to contend with this searing intrusion of racial trauma. At the same time, blackness is in our exchange the site of pride and privilege of its own. Gloria's question to me, “Are you really black?”, becomes, in time, a question that she puts to herself. Her questions cohere in a version of racial authenticity in which blackness has been reappropriated as a marker of loving inclusion.

Dr. Druck is author of Four Therapeutic Approaches to the Borderline Patient (1989).

This commentary addresses two themes: parallels between religious and psychoanalytic education and the question of group survival in a world of competitive groups, whether religious (“strict” vs. “weak”) or psychoanalytic (differing psychoanalytic approaches). “Strict” religious education involves teaching both critical thinking and identification with the particular religion. This blend of critical thinking and identification with psychoanalysis is crucial in psychoanalytic education. We want to graduate students who see themselves as psychoanalysts rather than as being “interested” in psychoanalysis. This goal is accomplished when students have close, positive experience with personal analysts, supervisors, and teachers who are strongly committed to psychoanalysis but in a manner that encourages students to think critically and find their own psychoanalytic perspective. With regard to the second theme, I discuss how our narcissistic commitment to one or another psychoanalytic model interferes with open integration of new insights. Individual analysts privately integrate competing ideas in their own idiosyncratic ways. When these individuals publicly represent competing psychoanalytic groups, however, they tend to emphasize differences among these groups. They then find ways to appropriate new ideas as extensions of their own evolving tradition. In this way, a

Three dreams from the first few years of an analysis of a significantly traumatized woman were selected to demonstrate their resonance with, organization of, and consolidation of certain pivotal themes emerging in the clinical process itself. These dreams occurred chronologically but not sequentially and are posited in the affective and thematic contexts in which they were reported. The dreams selected herald the beginning of a new phase in the patient's development, which had previously been mired in darkness and despair.

A Dialogue on Racial Melancholia

David Eng PHD and Shinhee Han CSW

Shinhee Han

New York, NY 10027
dle8@columbia.edu

New York, NY 10027
sh85@columbia.edu

Shinhee Han   Psychotherapist at the Counseling & Psychological Services of Columbia University. She is a doctoral candidate in the Shirley M. Ehrenkranz School of Social Work at New York University and maintains a private practice in New York City.

As Freud's privileged theory of unresolved grief, melancholia presents a compelling framework to conceptualize registers of loss and depression attendant to both psychic and material processes of Asian American immigration, assimilation, and racialization. Freud initially formulates melancholia as a pathological form of individual mourning for lost objects, places, or ideals. However, we propose a concept of melancholia as a depathologized structure of everyday group experience for Asian Americans. We analyze a number of Asian American cultural productions (literature and film) as well as two case histories of university students involving intergenerational conflicts and lost ideals of whiteness, Asianness, home, and language. Exploring these analyses against Klein's notions of lost objects, we propose a more refined theory of good and bad racialized objects. This theory raises the psychic and political difficulties of reinstatement and the mediation of the depressive position for Asian Americans. In addition, this theory suggests that processes of immigration, assimilation, and racialization are neither pathological nor permanent but involve the fluid negotiation between mourning and melancholia. Throughout this essay, we consider methods by which a more speculative approach to psychoanalytic theory and clinical practice might offer a deeper understanding of Asian American mental health issues.

We thank Neil Altman, Muriel Dimen, David Kazanjian, Tazuko Shibusawa, Carolyn Stack, Serena Volpp, and Sophie Volpp for their generous advice and expert help in the revision of this essay.

Steven Cooper PHD

Steven Cooper

Steven Cooper   Training and Supervising Analyst, Boston Psychoanalytic Institute and Society; Supervising Analyst and Faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; and Clinical Associate Professor of Psychology, Beth Israel Hospital Harvard Medical School.

The patient's containment of the analyst's affect occurs in a broad range of situations that extend far beyond the more visible instances when the analyst is directly expressive of his or her own affects. This paper begins to explore how patients help analysts contain various kinds of affects within the analytic process, particularly more routine and less heroic types of containment. Although this containment is generally a far less prominent feature of analytic work than is the containment provided by the analyst for the patient, it is omnipresent. Routine elements of containment that the patient provides for the analyst involve working with the knowledge of the limits of the other—including the possibility that in a long-term treatment the patient will often get to know quite well some of the quotidian aspects of the analyst's personality and its relation to the patient's conflicts. Mutual aspects of containment are extremely important in the expression and titration of anger and disappointment, desire, hope, humor, and the negotiation of psychic possibility within the analytic dyad.

Standing in the spaces: essays on clinical process, trauma, and dissociation by Philip M. Bromberg. (Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1998, 328 pp.)

THERE ARE FEW WRITERS I ENJOY READING AS MUCH AS I DO PHILIP Bromberg—or who teach me as much in the process. Like Harry Stack Sullivan, he has so mastered the brief, ironic, clinical narrative that the characters he brings to life stay in our minds as indelibly as did some of Sullivan's or Freud's. Further, his own personal engagements with us, those jokes with their sharp clinical edges, for example, continue to make their teaching points long after we have put the book down. I find myself repeatedly quoting his story about the man who smoked his own penis (p. 178), or the definitions he passes along for hysteria (“Someone who goes through life pretending to be who he really is” and “An hysteric is like a glass of water without the glass,” p. 223), along with his view of a self psychologist, unfair though it may be, as “an interpersonal analyst who is deaf in one ear” (p. 158).

I have for a while been grappling with Bromberg's view of dissociation and trying to sort out how to inhabit it without losing my self in the process. I am sure he is right about the experience of trying to “feel like one self while being many” (p. 12), as he is about the shard-like character of life itself. I particularly admire the ways he finds to describe this process without politicizing the discourse.

Over the past 10 years, i have spent a good deal of my professional life writing and speaking about the long-term effects of childhood sexual abuse; what constitutes psychological trauma; and which part of the experience so pervades and controts future psychological functioning and so undermines the development of positive, nurturing interpersonal relationships. In the course of this work, I have become convinced that the most devastating aspect of childhood abuse, particularly parental abuse, is not the physical overstimulation or the fantasied elaborations of these actual events (though these are of great significance). I have come to believe that the most singularly devastating aspect of childhood abuse is the violent penetration and coopting of mind that occurs when one is emotionally and physically dependent on another who violates and exploits—when, due to the simultaneously dependent and exploitive aspects, one person in a dyad is granted the authority to control and define the other's reality, even when that definition of reality exists in stark contrast to the person's actual lived experience.

An earlier version of this paper was presented as an Invited Address to the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) at the annual meeting of the American Psychological Association, San Francisco, August 1998, and at the Fourth International Conference on Sexual Misconduct by Psychotherapists, Other Health Care Professionals, and Clergy, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Boston, October 1998.

In both traditional and relational psychoanalytic conceptions of the treatment frame, the appearance of the person of the analyst (or therapist) from behind the wizard's curtain has accompanied a general acceptance of the shift from a “one-person” to a “two-person” psychology. As Abend (1995) stated at a White Institute plenary panel (at which he commented that he thought he had been invited as “a spokesman for the traditional, or classical position”), “Talk about a two-person model of analytic process—what other model is tenable?” (p. 208).

The question posed at this White Institute fiftieth anniversary conference, “Self-Disclosure: Therapeutic Tool or Indulgence,” reflects our common contemporary dilemma as we try to figure out what should or should not be going on between the two people inside the treatment frame. Greenberg (1995), presenting a clinical vignette along with a detailed rationale for why he had chosen to answer a patient's direct question with analytic silence, asserted: “Received technical prescriptions cannot help me in this moment with Mr. C; I must rely on what I know about him, myself, and about our relationship in deciding how to respond” (pp. 202-203). Thomas Ogden MD

Thomas Ogden   Co-Director, Center for the Advanced Study of the Psychoses, San Francisco; Supervising and Training Analyst, Psychoanalytic Institute of Northern California; and Faculty, San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute.

I will begin by gathering some of the threads of the fabric that Dr. jeanne wolff bernstein weaves in her remarkable paper on Shimon Attie's The Writing on the Wall. My commentary involves an effort to enter into conversation with a work of photographic art. The art in turn is an attempt to create an adequate response in the medium of photography to the artist's inarticulate feeling state evoked by all of the human suffering and human atrocity that are collected under the painfully inadequate word Holocaust.

Bernstein plays beautifully with the paradox that the most serious work we can do with our response to an event, to a set of feelings and memories, is to engage in play, in serious play, with them. Attie's The Writing on the Wall is a work of art in which the nature of the playing going on is, in large part, the act of creation of an experience of mourning. Implicit in Bernstein's paper is the notion that mourning the victims of the Holocaust, if it is to be adequate to the people who have been lost, must create something—a set of photographs, a poem, a song, a psychoanalytic paper, a conversation with one's spouse or child—something living that in some important way meets all that has been lost as well as the experience of loss itself.

Bernstein dexterously explores the ways in which a “memorializing place” is created by Attie's work. She stresses that, in making a memorial place, a “potential space” is created in which the viewer/ reader/listener has an opportunity, perhaps even a responsibility, to create his or her own response. A memorializing place is a creation of photographer and viewer, writer and reader, musician and listener. Few have described the act of cocreation involved in the experiencing of a work of art more succinctly and more beautifully than Borges (1964)

Donnel Stern   Training and Supervising Analyst at the William Alanson White Institute; Faculty at the New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis; author of Unformulated Experience (1997); and a member of the editorial boards of Contemporary Psychoanalysis, Psychoanalytic Dialogues, and Psychoanalytic Inquiry.

The insufficiencies that Joan Copjec finds in the work of Judith Butler are the same kind Dyess and Dean want to alert us to in relational psychoanalysis. Two dangers of this nature are reification (that is, the relational position's becoming “the Book”) and a flirtation with superficiality (a potential outcome of believing that all experience can be understood in the terms of social relatedness). Theorizing “the impossibility of meaning” may be a first step in addressing these problems without having to limit the terms of the discussion to nature and nurture, or essence and social construction. But the idea of the Real is inextricably interrelated with, and mutually defined by, other parts of Lacan's theory. And so, if we simply import into relational psychoanalysis Lacan's conception of the Real, we are mixing apples and oranges and thereby risking conceptual confusion. We should instead use Lacan's idea as inspiration for the construction of a conception of “the impossibility of meaning” that can be used in theorizing the particular kind of problems relational psychoanalysis sets itself.

Marcia Cavell   Visiting Associate Professor of Philosophy at the University of California, Berkeley; Interdisciplinary Member of the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; Academic Affiliate of the American Psychoanalytic Association; Candidate in Training at the San Francisco Psychoanalytic Institute; and author of The Psychoanalytic Mind: From Freud to Philosophy (Harvard University Press, 1993).

Standing in the spaces: essays on clinical process, trauma, and dissociation by Philip M. Bromberg. (Hillsdale, NJ: The Analytic Press, 1998, 328 pp.)

Janet (1889) viewed memory as the central organizing apparatus of the mind. His work with hysterics led him to think that memory pursues two different paths: It can function automatically, in a way that is shared by human beings and other animals, and it can function in a narrative way, integrating new experiences into existing mental schema. In the normal case, these two forms of memory cooperate so that we do not see them as separate tracks. But, because the possibility of narrative memory depends on the condition of the person at the time of the experience, and on the nature of the experience itself, trauma can make the tracks diverge. Although familiar experiences are automatically assimilated without much conscious attention, frightening or very novel ones may not become part of an integrating narrative at all. They are remembered in a way that is dissociated from conscious awareness.

As we know, Freud's early work was directly influenced by Janet. In “Studies on Hysteria,” Breuer and Freud (1893–1895) wrote that hysterics suffer mainly from reminiscences, by which they meant traumatic, nonnarrative memories.

Commentary on Paper by Hazel Ipp

Michael Adams CSW D.Phil.

Michael Adams

1 Washington Square Village, #5A
New York, NY 10012
adamsmv@aol.com

Michael Adams   Psychotherapist in New York City and an analyst in training at the C. G. Jung Institute. He teaches dream interpretation at the Object Relations Institute and at New School University. He is the author of The Multicultural Imagination: “Race,” Color and the Unconscious.

Jungians believe that compensation in the service of individuation is the primary transformative function of dreams. Jung (1916a) classifies dreams in three basic categories: reactive, compensatory, and prospective. Reactive dreams simply reproduce an experience that has had a traumatic emotional impact on the psyche. According to Jung, however, most dreams are compensatory. What they compensate is the attitude of the ego in the present. The attitude of the ego is always partial and prejudicial; in the extreme case, it may be utterly defective. Jung defines the ego as identity. That is, the ego is identified with a certain attitude and is disidentified from other, alternative perspectives of which it is, for whatever reason, unconscious. Compensatory dreams challenge the ego to relate to perspectives to which it has previously been unrelated or ineffectively related. The ego may then seriously entertain, evaluate, and either accept or reject these perspectives.

There is no imperative for the ego to integrate these perspectives. What Jung advocates is not uncritical captitulation by the ego to the unconscious but a retional dialogue between the ego and the unconscious.

This symposium addresses a psychoanalytic lacuna: race. The papers it comprises, drawn from both clinical and academic precincts, pick up the thread of social commentary that runs through the psychoanalytic fabric. Here Psychoanalytic Dialogues joins other like-minded efforts to position the psychic and the social, the clinical and the cultural, in the same discourse, and to consider the painfulness of political inequity amid therapeutic intimacy. These essays demonstrate the clinical and intellectual benefits of the engagement with the disciplines characteristic of psychoanalysis in the previous generation. They also reveal how the evolution of psychoanalytic practice itself potentiates recognition of psychoanalytically marginalized dimensions of society and culture that constitute subjectivity and inform everyday clinical life.

Gender

The Impossibility of Meaning

Cynthia Dyess MD and Tim Dean PHD

Tim Dean

1505 11th Avenue, Suite 306
Seattle, WA 98122
cdyess@connectexpress.com

dean@uiuc.edu

Tim Dean   Associate Professor of English and Interpretive Theory at the University of Illinois, Urbana-Champaign, and author of Gary Snyder and the American Unconscious (1991) and Beyond Sexuality (2000).

This paper offers an antiessentialist, psychoanalytic account of gender by arguing against Butler's deconstructive critique of gender essentialism. We develop an alternative to Butler's conception of gender as performative by focusing on those aspects of gender that resist meaning and representation. Using Lacan's concept of the real, we argue that any viable theory of gender must account for the limit conditions of cultural discourses that constitute subjectivity and sociality. Once gender is understood in terms not of proliferating possibilities for meaning, but of a certain impossibility of meaning, then gender's bearing on human relationality requires reconceptualizing. Claiming that various cultural narratives about gender (including traditional psychoanalytic narratives) should be recognized as symptomatic attempts to come to terms with a fundamental impossibility at the heart of sexual difference, we conclude by suggesting ways in which psychoanalysis may productively illuminate the failures of meaning that structure human relationality.

The experience of self is partially a narrative of one's history; without memory, there is no self. Central to personal histories are accounts of significant relationships and their successes and failures. Accounts of failed relationships tend to gravitate toward two narrative themes: selfpity and guilt. This essay explores some aspects of the nature of guilt and self-pity, the different forms in which they surface, and their relationship to each other.

There is a wide variety of positions subsumed under the rubric of interpersonal psychoanalysis. However, they all hold in common two main parameters of investigation. First, there is some variety of detailed inquiry into the patient's life (past, present, real, fantasied). Second, there is close attention to the nuances of the patient—therapist interactional field. The therapist is considered a coparticipant in the creation of that field. Interpersonalists differ in how actively and openly they use their own participation, but a meticulous self-monitoring of one's own contribution to the interpersonal field is the vis a tergo of interpersonal psychoanalysis (Gill, 1983; Levenson, 1985; Bromberg, 1989; Greenberg, 1991; Ehrenberg, 1995; Aron, 1996).

The detailed inquiry serves two separate functions. First, asking questions serves to deconstruct the patient's iconic myths, the prepackaged version of his or her life. The stories tend to fall apart as inquiry proceeds. In this sense, inquiry operates much like free association. The second function is essentially Sullivanian in intent. Sullivan (1954) believed that it is necessary to grasp the nuances of the patient's idiosyncratic life experience. Where and how one lived are important. Emphasis on the contextuality of a person's life conveys the richness of individual experience. Rather than knowing that the patient felt rejected by a cold mother, Sullivan would want to know how, where, and when these interactions occurred (Kvarnes and Parloff, 1976). The Psychoanalysis of Hypocrisy

Leo Rangell MD

Leo Rangell

456 North Carmelina Avenue

Leo Rangell   Honorary President, International Psychoanalytic Association, and Clinical Professor of Psychiatry, University of California, Los Angeles, and Uneiversity of California, San Francisco.

Many analysts look askance at any use of psychoanalysis in political life. This view is predicated on the opinion that one would invariably mix his analytic goals with his personal cognitive and affective predilections. I do not share this view and have combined an analytic observational stance with a long personal interest in the rampant psychopathology that accompanies political life. In addition to conducting a daily practice, I have observed and tried to understand analytically a series of emotionally charged public events, from the McCarthy era of the American social experience, through the Nixon presidency before and after Watergate, the Anita Hill-Clarence Thomas hearings before the Senate Judiciary Committee, trials of compelling national interest, such as the rape trial of William Kennedy Smith, the Tyson rape trial, the bombshell O. J. Simpson murder trial, and now, most recently, the conflict-ridden impeachment hearings against Bill Clinton, the popular president of the United States. Conflict centered as these galvanizing events proved to be, each riveting the attention of an absorbed public, there can be no argument about the place for their psychoanalytic understanding.

The present essay is therefore part of a larger ongoing investigation of the dynamic background behind public opinion (Rangell, in preparation) during emotional crises in national events. In all these studies, the object of observation has been a pyramidal social structure consisting of a leader or leaders at the top, the staff or bureaucracy under him, including elected representatives and the media, and the mass of people at the base of the population pyramid.

Jung, through his close collaboration with freud, was one of the founders of psychoanalysis. Subsequently breaking from Freud, he developed his own theories, which he called analytical psychology. In pioneering the development of his particular depth psychology, Jung cast an influence, albeit by and large outside psychoanalysis, that has been monumental and that has continued long past his death in 1961. Jung was convinced of the importance of prewired givens such as a central unconscious ordering principle of personality, which he called the self, and a fundamental driving force toward self-realization, a process he termed individuation (Jung, 1953, p. 182). In the clinical arena, Jung, anticipating some of our postmodern sensibilities, emphasized the mutual influence that occurs between patient and analyst and the required deep involvement of the analyst for analysis to work. Today, Jung's analytical psychology is experiencing a resurgence in interest and influence within the practice of psychotherapy and our culture at large.

The Organizing Functions of Dreaming–A Contemporary Psychoanalytic Model

Commentary on Paper by Hazel Ipp

James Fosshage PHD

James Fosshage

330 West 58th Street, Suite 200
New York, NY 10019
fosshage@psychoanalysis.net

James Fosshage   Co-founder and Board Director, National Institute for the Psychotherapies, New York City; Founding Faculty, Institute for the Psychoanalytic Study of Subjectivity, New York City; and Clinical Professor of Psychology, New York University Postdoctoral Program in Psychotherapy and Psychoanalysis.

Dream mentation, a process that occurs during sleep, centrally functions, as waking mentation does, to process information. When we dream, we use variably dual modes of cognition—the imagistic, sensory-dominated primary-process mode and the linguistically anchored secondary-process mode (Noy, 1969, 1979; McLaughlin, 1978; McKinnon, 1979; Fosshage, 1983, 1987a, b; Lichtenberg, 1983; Bucci, 1985, 1994). These modes appear in dreams in the form of sensory images and spoken and unspoken words. Just as words are placed in a logical, coherent order to shape meaning and cognitive focus, so too are images sequentially ordered to express meaning and to further affective-cognitive processing (Fosshage, 1983). Sensory images tend to evoke more affect (see Epstein, 1994, for a review), which clarifies why dreams (especially rapid eye movement [REM] dreams, which are more imagistically dominated as compared to non-REM [NREM] dreams) can be so emotionally powerful. Although contemporary dream models differ, the view of dreams as centrally processing information is, I believe, an increasingly convergent perspective evolving out of dream and psychophysiological research, contemporary psychoanalytic theory, cognitive psychology, and clinical work.

I refer to my particular model as the organization model of dreams because the core process and function of dreaming is to organize data.

Julie Gerhardt  

having come to appreciate the analytic possibilities of first thoughts, what first came to mind when presented with Benjamin's theoretical distinction between “personhood or subjectivity” as opposed to “subjecthood” will be shared. These terms do double duty for Benjamin. Not only do they serve as shorthand to distinguish “between the patient's recognizing the analyst's subjectivity (personhood) and recognizing the analyst's independent existence as outside and different from the patient's mental fantasy (subjecthood)” (Benjamin), but their very duality harbors an implicit hierarchization. “Subjecthood” is to “personhood” as male is to female—some things always end up on top! We have apparently joined the ranks of those expressive personhood types—or perhaps those practical types with a “technical focus”—rather than appreciate the “structural aspects of the intersubjective dialectic” inherent in true subjecthood. Is this yet another effort to reinscribe a dualistic rendering of the “what” and the “that” of the matter?

What first came to to mind when confronted with this distinction was a paradox from Wittgenstein (1958) about the nature of representation. Conjure any image: Is it intended as a representation of something that is or was or of something yet to be? Conjure a picture of world peace: Is it a nostalgic vision of a bygone world or an apocalyptic vision of the future? Similarly, in Austin's (1962) celebrated discussion of speech acts as representational media, he singles out the “performative” as a type of speech, the very utterance of which makes something so. The proverbial example, “I hereby pronounce you husband and wife,” is used not to represent a preexisting state of affairs—in contrast to “The cat is on the mat,” which is—but to actually bring about marital union.

[[RunningHead]]

Introduction to Panel

The Role of Consultations in the Prevention of Boundary Violations

Stuart Pizer ABPP

Stuart Pizer

Stuart Pizer   President-elect, Supervising Analyst and Faculty, Massachusetts Institute for Psychoanalysis; Clinical Instructor in Psychology, Harvard Medical School; Founding Chair of the Ethics Committee of the Psychoanalytic Consortium; and a Contributing Editor of Psychoanalytic Dialogues.

This panel speaks to the vulnerability of the clinician and to the responsibility and integrity with which it is managed. A symposium on the value of consultations was conceived by the Ethics Committee of the Psychoanalytic Consortium as a consciousness-raising project. The symposium was presented as an invited panel of the Division of Psychoanalysis (Division 39) at the annual meeting of the American Psychological Association, San Francisco, August 1998, and then as a component of the Fourth International Conference on Sexual Misconduct by Psychotherapists, Other Health Care Professionals, and Clergy, Boston Psychoanalytic Society and Institute, Boston, October 1998. The Ethics Committee seeks to promote, among all professional mental health providers, a climate of awareness of ethical principles, and the continual practice of self-monitoring necessitated by each clinican's ongoing susceptibility to ethical conflicts and dilemmas.

Mindful of how the privacy and intensity of the analytic process challenge every analyst to manage highly evocative, destabilizing, or enticing moments in the treatment relationship, we believe that the establishment of a regular practice of consultations—(perhaps two or three a year) by all analysts (and, ultimately, all professional psychotherapists) would support the individual practitioner to maintain a check on personal vulnerabilities, blind spots, or interactive lapses in the clinical work.

[[RunningHead]]

A White Therapist, an African American Patient—Shame in the Therapeutic Dyad

Commentary on Paper by Neil Altman

Janice Gump PHD

Janice Gump

4545 42nd Street, Suite 304
Washington DC 20016
jangump@aol.com

Janice Gump   Maintains a private practice in Washington, DC, teaches at the Washington School of Psychiatry, and is a member of the Institute of Contemporary Psychotherapy of Washington, DC.

Neil altman's candid sharing of this daunting case reveals a courage and openness too rarely encountered. It is difficult to talk honestly about race when our feelings depart from “correctness.” Such a dialogue is long overdue.

Although some liberal bias may exist in many centers of professional training, the professional, like everyone else, is profoundly shaped by the culture's constructions of reality. A disturbing example of such influence is provided by a recent Georgetown University study. Experimenters (Schulman et al., 1999) sought to determine the role of physician decision-making in race and sex differences found in the treatment of cardiovascular disease. A group of 720 primary care physicians were given simulated patient protocols from which they were to determine need for further cardiac diagnostic procedures. Cardiac catheterization is considered the most sophisticated and useful of these procedures, “the best way to diagnose coronary artery disease and to decide whether to perform cardiac surgery” (Goldstein, 1999, p. A13). Effects of six variables were assessed: patient gender, race, age, level of coronary risk, type of chest pain, and results of an exercise stress test. Interviewed “patients” (videotaped actors) read from identical scripts, wore identical clothes, and had the same symptoms, insurance, occupations, and risk factors.

Catheterization was recommended for blacks (both male and female) 60 percent as often as it was prescribed for whites (male and female).

Portions of this paper were presented at the American Psychological Association Public Interest Directorate's Miniconvention on Psychology and Racism, August 15, 1997.

responseSet.title: Post-Jungian Dialogues
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Post-Jungian Dialogues

Andrew Samuels D.H.L

Andrew Samuels

148 Mercers Road,
London, N19 4PX
England
andrew.samuels@virgin.net

Andrew Samuels   Professor of Analytical Psychology, University of Essex; a Jungian training analyst, lecturer, and political consultant; and the author of numerous books including Jung and the Post-Jungians and the forthcoming The Secret Life of Politics.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Jung's Insistence that the Analyst be “IN” the Treatment Just as much as the patient is was a clinical turning point. Although there is now overwhelming evidence that, in many respects, this could also be said to be Freud's position in practice, I think it would be true to say that the Freudian tradition over the years has not been so interactively or relationally based. Once the analyst is in the treatment on an open, regular, even official basis, it is possible to begin to theorize—again, openly, regularly, and officially—about this phenomenon. Hence, Jungian analysis has been for a long time a rather good laboratory in which the practitioner's role has been scrutinized more throughly than in other schools of depth-psychological therapy.

But of course analytical therapy is more than an interactional or relational process. Each participant has inside a ceaseless conscious-unconscious dynamic, which means that there are at least three important relations to consider—the interactive one and the conscious-unconscious dynamics of the two participants. Moreover, all of this is going on simultaneously. (There is a famous diagram, famous at least in Jungian and post-Jungian circles, that tries to give the flavor of this simultaneity. In a moment, I present my own particular version of this diagram.)

First I want to emphasize another of Jung's clinical principles, which is that the analyst is almost always a “wounded healer.” Here, I believe there is more at stake than the conventional view that personal neuroses drive one to become a shrink.

responseSet.authorMast: Neil Altman and  Jody Messler Davies
responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Editorial

Neil Altman and Jody Davies

Jody Davies

Jody Davies  

As we officially assume the editorship of Psychoanalytic Dialogues, we face one of those junctures at which it seems natural to stop and reflect on the journey thus far—the accomplishments and the frustrations—as well as on future directions. At this time, we want to reach out to you, our readers, who have always seemed to us more of a community defined by a shared vision than a simple roster of subscribers. This vision was a project, in the shape of a journal—a project to breathe vitality into psychoanalytic discourse, to recognize creative thinking, to empower those with the courage to confront difficult questions while maintaining the intellectual rigor and academic standards required for theoretical evolution and advancement. It has been an active project, shared with all of you. In the evolution of this journal, many of the doubtful have become readers, readers have become contributors, contributors have become editors, and the face of psychoanalysis has changed in profound ways.

As we enter the journal's second decade of publication, our commitment to intellectual creativity, innovation, and rigor will remain unchanged. But we recognize that, at the beginning of its second hundred years, psychoanalysis is in a particularly paradoxical place. Intellectually, our field has never been so rich, so alive with well reasoned disagreement and debate, so free from the constraints of tradition for tradition's sake, so ready to ask the kind of bold questions that will prepare psychoanalysis to secure its own future. But as a therapeutic modality, a treatment option for those most in need, psychoanalysis stands more threatened than at any other time in its history.

Some within our field believe that the survival of psychoanalysis depends on a demonstration of political unity—on de-emphasizing theoretical diversity and debate in an attempt to produce a “united front” to the purveyors of managed care and health insurance benefits. Some suggest that highlighting differences among us is somehow dangerous to the future of our discipline.

[[RunningHead]]

Classical Jung Meets Klein and Bion

JoAnn Culbert-Koehn L.C.S.W.

JoAnn Culbert-Koehn

9730 Wilshire Boulevard #114
Beverly Hills, CA 90212

JoAnn Culbert-Koehn   Jungian analyst in private practice with adults and children in Beverly Hills, CA. She is a former Director of Training at the C. G. Jung Institute of Los Angeles and former Co-Director of the Hilde Kirsch Children's Center. She has published and lectured in the United States and in Europe on issues of separation and birth trauma and has taught in an innovative program in Santa Fe, NM, integrating Jung, Klein, and Bion.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Several Years Ago, I made a List of what I Thought were the important concepts from my Jungian training that were still of value to me in my practice as a Jungian analyst. I asked myself, “What marks my clinical work and my way of thinking as a Jungian?”

A profound respect for the inner world, for psychic reality.

Experience of the existence of both a personal and collective unconscious.

Experience of the religious function of the psyche.

Experiences of certain archetypal patterns that influenced my existence—initiatory ritual, mother—daughter separation, expulsion from paradise, sibling rivalry, death and rebirth, and alchemical processes.

Experience of the relationship between ego and Self. Seeing the psyche structured this way—ego enriched by Self, Self informed by ego, Self as the center of personality. Jung's idea of the psyche with two centers (ego and Self) is perhaps his most important contribution.

Viewing psyche as having certain structures—ego, Self, persona, shadow, anima, animus.

Interest in typology. Valuing of different personality types; valuing introversion as well as extraversion; valuing thinking, feeling, intuition, and sensation.

responseSet.abstract:
[[RunningHead]]

Answers to Nine Questions about Jungian Psychology

David Sedgwick PHD

David Sedgwick

205 East High Street
Charlottesville, VA 22902
dwsedgwick@aol.com

David Sedgwick   Jungian analyst in private practice in Charlottesville, Virginia. He is a member of the Interregional Society of Jungian Analysts and the author of Jung and Searles: A Comparative Study; The Wounded Healer: Countertransference from a Jungian Perspective; Jungian Psychotherapy: The Therapeutic Relationship (forthcoming); and numerous articles and book reviews.

Jung's Most Significant Clinical Contributions

Jung's Chief Original Contribution to Analytic Practice was his emphasis on the analyst's involvement in the therapeutic situation. Specifically, Jung was prescient in emphasizing the countertransference dimensions in therapy, the inevitability of a psychological intermingling of patient and analyst at an unconscious level, the primary therapeutic importance therefore of “the doctor's personality” and vulnerability, and thus the need for analysts to themselves be analyzed. Jung (1958) stated that transference is “the crux, or at any rate the crucial experience, in any thoroughgoing analysis” (p. vii), and Jung meant by transference a mutual, psychological interpenetration in depth (i.e., transference and countertransference in complex conjunction). In practice, many Jungians maintain this primary focus on the therapeutic dyad. Brief illustrations of all this are not feasible, but longer examples from my own practice can be found in a book I wrote on countertransference (Sedgwick, 1994).

Another significant contribution was Jung's approach to and use of dreams, fantasies, and metaphor. This usage is predicated on his hypothesis of a creative, collective unconscious that could generate symbolic images of either personal or archetypal (nonpersonal) quality. Most Jungians tend to respect these unconscious images in their manifest forms, not boiling them down immediately but approaching them, if possible, without preconception or, failing that, with a positive anticipation of an unfolding meaning.
